cataloging_subject.label: Libeaus Desconus
cataloging_subject.scope_note: The cataloged unit is the anonymous Middle English Arthurian tail-rhyme romance conventionally titled Libeaus Desconus. It includes the abstract work represented by six textually divergent witnesses but keeps individual manuscript copies, proposed recensions, nineteenth- and twenty-first-century editions, working transcripts, the U-M encoding, the repository XML, and facsimiles as distinct layers. The extent of 186 stanzas and 2,232 lines describes Max Kaluza's reconstructed 1890 critical text as encoded in CME00144, not an invariant archetypal extent and not any one manuscript's line count. Kaluza's German and Latin apparatus and 301 embedded alternate-witness lines are edition-level material and do not make the medieval work multilingual or 2,533 lines long.

catalog_summary.title: Libeaus Desconus
catalog_summary.author: Anonymous
catalog_summary.date: Composed circa 1325-1350; Cotton Caligula A II and Lincoln's Inn Hale 150 are dated circa 1400 in the METS introduction, with conflicting institutional and database dates preserved; Kaluza's critical edition was published in 1890

titles.supplied_by: General Corpus from the conventional scholarly title
Variant conventional spelling in scholarship and witness cataloging

titles.value: Libeaus Desconus: Die mittelenglische Romanze vom Schönen unbekannten

scope.description: Bibliographic edition title incorporating the conventional Middle English work title

assertion.notes: The German subtitle belongs to Kaluza's edition layer rather than to the medieval work's language or original title.

titles.value: Ihesu Crist, our saviour, And his modir, that swete flour

assertion.notes: DIMEV normalizes the opening as 'Jesu Christ our savior'; this record preserves the source XML spelling without treating it as invariant across witnesses.

assertion.notes: Witness endings differ; this is the explicit of the represented critical reading text.

agents.name: Thomas Chestre
notes: Historically proposed as author; current scholarship does not regard the attribution as established.

agents.name: Max Kaluza
notes: Editor of the immediate 1890 source edition encoded by U-M and CME00144.

agents.name: August Rhode
notes: Prepared working copies of the Lincoln's Inn, Ashmole, Lambeth, and Cotton texts later checked throughout by Kaluza.

agents.name: Eugen Kölbing
notes: Collated the Naples witness in print and supplied Kaluza with an unlocated complete private transcript.

agents.name: John W. Hales and Frederick J. Furnivall
notes: Collective display agent for the joint 1867-1868 volume; individual editors remain separately identified.

agents.name: Eve Salisbury and James Weldon
notes: Collective display agent for their joint 2013 edition; individual editors remain separately identified.

assertion.notes: No medieval author is securely identified. Later editors, collators, and transcribers remain separate responsibilities.
The historical attribution is retained for retrieval, not accepted as established authorship.

scope.description: Immediate 1890 eclectic critical edition represented by CME00144

notes: Kaluza reconstructed a reading text from six witnesses, supplied conjectural metrical readings, and normalized several scribal features.

scope.description: Working copies of I, A, L, and C used and fully rechecked by Kaluza

notes: Rhode's copies are material intermediaries, not medieval witnesses or coauthorship of the work.

scope.description: Naples witness collation and complete private transcript used by Kaluza

notes: The 1877 article is a published variant collation, while Kaluza separately reports a complete private transcript.

responsibilities.display_name: John W. Hales and Frederick J. Furnivall

scope.description: Published Percy Folio text used by Kaluza, with supplementary witness endings

notes: The joint display name avoids assigning every act in the volume to one editor; the lineage manifest preserves the exact edition-to-witness relation.

scope.description: Modern 2013 comparative edition of separate Lambeth and Naples texts

notes: Their edition is a downstream scholarly representation, not the source of CME00144.

assertion.notes: The Middle English Romance database gives circa 1325-1350, while the METS introduction uses circa 1350; the broader range is retained rather than presenting a single year as exact.

date_statements.display: Cotton Caligula A II and Lincoln's Inn Hale MS 150 each dated circa 1400 in the METS introduction

assertion.notes: This is a joint qualified attestation window because the evidence does not establish which witness is earlier. METS dates both item texts circa 1400; its Cotton date conflicts with the current BL component record's second-half-fifteenth-century date and the Middle English Romance database's circa 1446-1460 date, while the Lincoln viewer gives a broader fourteenth-century description and leaves composite/refoliation history unresolved. These source-specific claims remain separate in lineage rather than being silently reconciled.

places.label: South-East Midlands
notes: A work-level composition localization from current romance scholarship; it is not inferred from any one witness's present holding or later dialect.

assertion.notes: The database assigns South-East Midlands. Witness dialects, including the Naples copy's southwestern associations, are separate evidence layers and do not relocate the abstract work automatically.
German editorial commentary, Latin abbreviations and colophon material, and modern English introductions belong to editions or witnesses rather than making the abstract romance multilingual.

form_statements.description: Narrative verse organized primarily in twelve-line tail-rhyme stanzas.

assertion.notes: The hero is Gawain's son, receives his name and quests at Arthur's court, and operates within Arthurian romance conventions.
The label identifies a narrative tradition and group of analogues, not proof of direct descent from a specific extant French text.

subject_statements.label: Quest, knightly testing, combat, and tournament
King Arthur, Gawain, and the Round Table

scope.description: The enchanted Lady of Synadoun appears as a dragon and is restored through the hero's kiss.

subject_statements.label: Travel through forests, courts, cities, gardens, and other secular spaces

summaries.value: Gingelein, the illegitimate son of Gawain, grows up ignorant of his name and lineage. After taking armor and arriving at Arthur's court, he receives the name Libeaus Desconus, the Fair Unknown, and undertakes a series of quests that test his prowess against knights, giants, and magical adversaries. His principal adventure takes him to Synadoun, where a lady transformed into a dragon is freed by his kiss. The romance concludes with recognition, reward, and marriage, joining Arthurian chivalry to the Fair Unknown pattern of concealed identity and earned social standing.

Six witnesses preserve substantially divergent forms and lengths: Lincoln's Inn Hale 150, Bodleian Ashmole 61, Lambeth 306, Cotton Caligula A II, the Percy Folio Add 27879, and Naples XIII.B.29. This record catalogs their shared abstract romance without accepting Kaluza's eclectic reconstruction, Mills's proposed CL/ANP recensions, or the 2013 Lambeth/Naples presentation as the only possible work boundary.

CME00144 encodes Kaluza's 1890 edition as one poem division with 186 direct LG elements and 2,232 direct L elements. Forty-two additional LG and 301 additional L elements are nested inside NOTE apparatus as alternate-witness material. The direct-child counts define this record's edition-specific extent; descendant-wide counts are not work extent. Kaluza's German notes, Latin phrases, page concordances, and title matter remain edition or encoding features rather than work-level language, form, or content parts.

extent.description: Kaluza's represented critical reading text comprises 186 numbered twelve-line stanzas and 2,232 direct verse lines in the sole BODY/DIV1 poem division. The count excludes the title page, headings, milestones, 2,272 NOTE elements, and the 42 LG/301 L elements embedded inside apparatus notes. Witness-specific extents range from the fragmentary 1,107-line Lincoln text to the 2,250-line Ashmole text and must not be summed or normalized to this critical-edition total.

content_parts.label: Libeaus Desconus
content_parts.summary: The complete 186-stanza critical reading text follows the Fair Unknown hero from concealed upbringing and arrival at Arthur's court through knightly quests, the disenchantment of the Lady of Synadoun, recognition, and marriage.

related_works.title: Li Biaus Descouneus
notes: Renaut de Bage's late-twelfth-century French romance is the closest surviving analogue. METS treats the extant French poem as an analogue; similar plot does not by itself prove that it was the direct exemplar.

related_works.title: Wigalois
notes: A Fair Unknown analogue used for comparative study, not a demonstrated direct source.

related_works.title: Carduino
notes: An Italian Fair Unknown analogue; relation is thematic and traditional rather than a confirmed direct lineage.

related_works.title: The Tale of Sir Gareth of Orkney
notes: Malory's Gareth narrative participates in the Fair Unknown tradition but is not treated as the source of this earlier Middle English romance.

open_questions.question: Is there any external evidence sufficient to establish or decisively reject the historical attribution of Libeaus Desconus to Thomas Chestre?
next_steps: Review current authorship scholarship and the transmission history of the attribution.
Compare linguistic and stylistic arguments with securely attributable texts without treating resemblance as external proof.
Retain Anonymous as the preferred responsibility and Thomas Chestre as disputed until stronger evidence appears.

open_questions.question: Does Libeaus Desconus derive directly from an identifiable French text, a lost French version, or a looser Fair Unknown narrative tradition?
next_steps: Compare the full Middle English witness tradition with Li Biaus Descouneus and other Fair Unknown analogues.
Separate shared motifs, common ancestry, direct translation, and independent adaptation.
Do not model the extant French romance or a hypothetical lost text as a confirmed direct source without demonstrable textual evidence.

open_questions.question: How securely do proposed CL and ANP recensions represent historical textual branches rather than editorial groupings, and can either shorter or longer form be treated as authorial?
next_steps: Collate all six witnesses at shared and divergent passages using complete images where available.
Test proposed shared errors and interpolations independently of Kaluza's C/z/P2 grouping.
Preserve recensional labels as scholarly hypotheses until the evidence supports a stable stemmatic model.

open_questions.question: Can the circa 1325-1350 date and South-East Midlands composition localization be narrowed without conflating work origin with later witness dialects?
next_steps: Review current metrical, lexical, dialectal, and intertextual dating arguments.
Distinguish authorial language reconstructed across witnesses from individual scribal dialects and copy dates.
Retain the current range and regional confidence until newer consensus evidence is found.

open_questions.question: How should witness-specific line-count discrepancies, especially Naples, and the two published normalized rhyme-pattern strings be reconciled?
next_steps: Compare DIMEV's 2,230-line Naples count with Kaluza's historical 2,275 and METS numbering, separating post-explicit moral lines and the Latin colophon.
Align stanza positions and rhyme sounds before interpreting different letter strings as different formal analyses.
Keep 2,232 lines and 186 stanzas explicitly scoped to Kaluza's critical reading text and CME00144's direct poem elements.

notes: Abstract work, proposed recension, six manuscript witnesses, nineteenth-century working transcripts and collations, scholarly editions, digital encoding, repository artifact, and facsimiles are modeled as distinct layers.
The XML's TEXT/@LANG value includes Middle English and German for the edition object, but the cataloged medieval work language is Middle English only.
The edition-specific 186-stanza/2,232-line extent is computationally reproducible from direct BODY/DIV1 children and independently reported for Kaluza by DIMEV.
The six witness line counts are not additive measures of work extent: A 2,250; C 2,130; P 2,241; L 2,204; I 1,107 and very imperfect; N 2,230 in current DIMEV.
Narrative analogues are related works, not silently promoted to direct sources; the lost French antecedent remains a hypothesis rather than an entity.
